title: Meet Your Brain
brainConcept: Your brain can grow and change when you practice (neuroplasticity).
objective: I can point to where my brain works and say that my brain gets stronger when I practice.
materials: None (optional: make a fist to model the brain).

flow.connect: Welcome the group. Everyone wiggles fingers and taps the top of their head: 'That's where your amazing brain lives!' Smile and say we are starting Brain Lab, where we learn how our brain helps us.
flow.teach: Make a fist: thumb tucked inside = the 'feeling part' (downstairs), fingers over it = the 'thinking part' (upstairs). When we are calm, the upstairs helps us think. Big news: your brain grows new connections every time you try something hard. Mistakes help it grow!
flow.practice: Practice a brand-new skill together (snap, wink, or pat-pat-clap pattern). Try it, miss it, laugh, try again. Say together: 'My brain is growing right now!'
flow.close: Each child shows the brain fist and says one word for how their brain feels today (sleepy, happy, ready).

contentQuestions.text: Where in your body is your brain?
What happens to your brain when you practice something new?
How did it feel when you missed the new move and tried again?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what is our name for the part of the brain that grows when we practice?
What is one thing you practiced this week to make your brain stronger?
Show me the brain fist and tell me which part is thinking and which is feeling.

title: Spot Your Signals
brainConcept: Attention is a spotlight you can aim, and your body sends signals about feelings.
objective: I can name a feeling and one thing my body does when I feel it.

flow.connect: Echo: 'Last time we learned our brain can ___ (grow)!' Then play 10 seconds of Spotlight: everyone aims their eyes and points at one object you name.
flow.teach: Your brain has a spotlight that points at what you care about. It also reads your body: a happy belly, hot cheeks when mad, wobbly tummy when nervous. Those are signals telling you how you feel.
flow.practice: Name a feeling (happy, mad, scared, excited). Kids show it with their face and point to where they feel it in their body. Repeat for 3 feelings.
flow.close: Each child says: 'Right now I feel ___ and my body feels it in my ___.'

contentQuestions.text: What does your body do when you feel happy? When you feel mad?
Where do you feel butterflies when you are nervous?
How can your body help you know how you feel?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what do we call the part of your brain that points at what you care about?
Tell me about a time your body gave you a feeling signal this week.
What feeling is the easiest for you to spot in your body?

title: Catch the Wave
brainConcept: Naming a big feeling helps the calm-down part of the brain take over.
objective: I can use one calm-down tool when a big feeling starts.

flow.connect: Echo: kids name one feeling and where they feel it. Then everyone makes a slow 'wave' with their hand rising up and coming back down.
flow.teach: Big feelings rise like a wave. If we notice early (using yesterday's body signals), we can ride the wave instead of getting knocked over. A great tool: name it. Saying 'I feel mad' helps the thinking brain come back.
flow.practice: Teach 'flower and candle' breathing: smell the flower (breathe in), blow the candle (breathe out), 4 times slowly. Then practice: 'When I feel big, I say my feeling and I breathe.'
flow.close: Each child picks: flower-candle breathing OR naming the feeling, and says when they will use it.

contentQuestions.text: What does it feel like in your body right before a big feeling?
Why is it easier to calm down when you catch the wave early?
Which calm-down tool feels best to you?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what is one calm-down tool we practiced?
Tell me about a time you used your tool this week. What happened?
Where is a good spot at school to take your flower-candle breaths?

title: Aim Your Spotlight
brainConcept: You can move your attention spotlight on purpose, even when something is distracting.
objective: I can put my attention spotlight on one thing and bring it back when it wanders.
materials: None (optional: a small object to focus on).

flow.connect: Echo: everyone shows a calm-down tool from last time. Then a 10-second 'spotlight on my hands' freeze.
flow.teach: Your spotlight wanders — that's normal! The skill is noticing it wandered and bringing it back, like a puppy on a leash. Every time you bring it back, your brain gets stronger at focus.
flow.practice: Play 'Spotlight Coach': stare at one object for 20 seconds. When your mind wanders, whisper 'come back' and look again. Do it twice.
flow.close: Each child says one place at school they need their spotlight (carpet time, lining up, reading).

contentQuestions.text: What pulls your spotlight away when you are trying to focus?
What can you say to yourself to bring your spotlight back?
When is focusing hardest for you?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what do we do when our spotlight wanders?
Tell me about a time you brought your spotlight back this week.
What is one trick that helps you focus?

title: Read the Room
brainConcept: Your brain reads faces and bodies to guess how others feel.
objective: I can look at a face or body and guess how someone might feel.

flow.connect: Echo: kids show where they aim their spotlight to focus. Then everyone makes a 'happy face' and a 'sad face.'
flow.teach: Your brain is a feelings-detective. It reads faces, voices, and bodies to guess how someone feels. Slumped shoulders might mean sad; big smile might mean happy.
flow.practice: Leader acts out a feeling with face and body (no words). Kids guess and point to the clue that helped them (mouth, eyes, shoulders). Do 3 rounds.
flow.close: Each child names one clue they will look for to read how a friend feels.

contentQuestions.text: What clues tell you someone is sad? Happy? Mad?
Can two people feel different things at the same time?
How does it help to know how a friend feels?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: name one face clue that shows a feeling.
Tell me about a time you noticed how a friend felt this week.
Who is someone whose feelings you are good at reading?

title: Walk in Their Shoes
brainConcept: Your brain can imagine what someone else is thinking or wanting.
objective: I can guess what someone else might want or feel in a situation.

flow.connect: Echo: kids name a feeling clue from last time. Then everyone imagines: 'Pretend you dropped your ice cream — show your face.'
flow.teach: Your brain can pretend to be in someone else's shoes. When we imagine how they feel, we connect it to a time we felt that way (that's the Velcro — sticking new to what we know).
flow.practice: Tell a tiny story (a new kid stands alone at recess). Kids answer: How might the new kid feel? What might help? Connect to: 'When did you feel left out?'
flow.close: Each child finishes: 'I can help someone feel better by ___.'

contentQuestions.text: How might the new kid in the story feel?
When have you felt the same way?
What is one kind thing that would help?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what does it mean to walk in someone's shoes?
Tell me about a time you helped someone feel included this week.
Who could you invite to play tomorrow?

title: Say It So They Hear It
brainConcept: Words stick better when they connect to what the listener already knows (Velcro).
objective: I can use my words to tell someone what I need in a kind way.

flow.connect: Echo: kids share one kind thing they did. Then practice a calm 'inside voice' all together.
flow.teach: When we want something, our words work better than grabbing or yelling. A simple shape: 'I feel ___, can you ___?' Calm words help the other person's brain listen.
flow.practice: Role-play: someone took your crayon. Practice: 'I feel sad, can I have it back please?' Each child tries with a partner.
flow.close: Each child shares one 'I feel ___, can you ___?' they could use.

contentQuestions.text: What happens when we grab or yell instead of using words?
Why do calm words help someone listen?
What is a kind way to ask for something back?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what are the two parts of our asking shape?
Tell me about a time you used your words this week.
What is something you might need to ask for tomorrow?

title: Fix-It Talk
brainConcept: Practicing how to make up strengthens the memory so it is easier next time (Echo).
objective: I can say sorry and offer a way to fix a problem with a friend.

flow.connect: Echo: kids show the asking shape from last time. Then everyone practices a friendly 'I'm sorry' voice.
flow.teach: Everyone bumps into problems with friends. A Fix-It Talk has 3 steps: say what happened, say sorry, offer to make it better ('Want to play together?'). Practicing it makes it easy to remember.
flow.practice: Role-play: you knocked over a friend's blocks. Walk through the 3 steps with a partner. Switch roles.
flow.close: Each child names the 3 Fix-It steps on their fingers.

contentQuestions.text: What are the three steps of a Fix-It Talk?
Why does saying sorry help a friendship?
What can you offer to make a problem better?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: say the three Fix-It steps.
Tell me about a problem you fixed with a friend this week.
Who might you need to have a Fix-It Talk with soon?

title: Stop & Plan
brainConcept: The thinking brain (upstairs) helps you stop and plan before you act.
objective: I can stop, think, and pick a good choice before I act.

flow.connect: Echo: kids name the Fix-It steps. Then play red light / green light for 30 seconds (practice stopping).
flow.teach: Before we act, our thinking brain can say STOP. Then we plan: 'What could I do? What might happen?' Stopping gives the upstairs brain time to help.
flow.practice: Scenario: you want a turn on the swing but a friend is on it. Stop. Name 2 choices. Pick the kind one. Do a second scenario together.
flow.close: Each child says their STOP word or signal they will use.

contentQuestions.text: Why is it smart to stop before you act?
What are two choices in the swing problem?
Which choice is kind and safe?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what do we do first before making a choice?
Tell me about a time you stopped and planned this week.
What is a STOP signal you can use in class?

title: Weigh It Out
brainConcept: Your brain can imagine what might happen next (mental time travel).
objective: I can think about what might happen after a choice.

flow.connect: Echo: kids share their STOP signal. Then everyone makes a 'thinking face.'
flow.teach: Your brain can peek into the future! Before choosing, ask: 'What might happen if I do this?' A good choice helps you and others. We connect it to times choices went well or not (Velcro).
flow.practice: Scenario: a friend dares you to push someone. Imagine what happens after pushing vs. walking away. Compare the two endings together.
flow.close: Each child finishes: 'A good choice helps ___.'

contentQuestions.text: What might happen if you push someone?
What might happen if you walk away?
Which ending is better and why?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what question do we ask before choosing?
Tell me about a good choice you made this week.
How did you know it was a good choice?

title: Put It Together
brainConcept: Connecting many small skills builds one strong web in your brain.
objective: I can use more than one Brain Lab tool in a pretend problem.

flow.connect: Echo round: each child shouts out one tool we learned (breathe, name it, spotlight, Fix-It, STOP).
flow.teach: All our tools connect like Velcro into one strong web. In a real problem you might use a few: notice your feeling, breathe, then make a smart choice.
flow.practice: Big scenario: someone cuts in line and you feel mad. Walk through it as a group: spot the feeling, calm down, use words, pick a choice.
flow.close: Each child picks the two tools they like best.

contentQuestions.text: Which tools could help when someone cuts in line?
What would you do first?
Which tool is your favorite?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: name three Brain Lab tools.
Tell me about a time you used two tools together this week.
Which tool do you want to practice more?

title: Rewire & Reflect
brainConcept: Practicing again and again over time makes brain connections strong and lasting.
objective: I can name a tool I will keep practicing to make my brain stronger.
materials: None (optional: a small certificate or sticker).

flow.connect: Echo party: go around twice, each child names a tool until we run out.
flow.teach: Your brain got stronger in Brain Lab because you practiced! The secret is to keep going — practice over time makes the connection stick (Rewire). Today we pick one tool to keep.
flow.practice: Each child sets a 'Rewire goal': one tool to use this week and where. Group cheers each goal.
flow.close: Celebrate! Everyone does the brain fist and says: 'My brain is growing!' Hand out certificates if available.

contentQuestions.text: What makes a brain connection strong and lasting?
Which tool helped you the most?
Where will you use your tool this week?

followupQuestions.text: Echo: what is your Rewire goal?
Tell me about how your brain grew during Brain Lab.
Who can you teach one of your tools to?
